column36: ✓No injectable prices were visible in the supplied website content, so all core aesthetics prices above are estimated from their beauty-salon positioning, non-medical practitioner profile, and typical Hampshire budget-to-lower-mid aesthetics pricing. Essenziale is likely to sit below nurse-led Winchester clinics on injectables: anti-wrinkle is likely around £140/£180/£220 versus a more typical nurse-led Winchester range of roughly £170-£220/£220-£280/£250-£320, and 1ml lips likely around £190 versus £220-£300+ in stronger medical clinics. They compete primarily on convenience, familiarity, and bundled beauty/aesthetics value rather than clinical authority or premium outcomes; the real threat is not high-end patient capture but price-sensitive salon clients who want everything under one roof. For a new premium clinic, this is not your direct benchmark on price, but it is a local anchor that can make your pricing look expensive unless you very clearly sell medical credentials, prescribing governance, complication management, and superior facial assessment as the reason for the gap.

column49: This is the most strategically relevant competitor for Abi. column49: The most interesting competitor in the analysis from a strategic perspective. Cathy Wallwork has credentials that dwarf every other nurse-led competitor assessed — she helped write the rules that everyone else plays by. Yet she is almost invisible online. This creates an unusual dynamic: she has deep loyalty from existing clients but is not discoverable to new ones. Abi's opportunity here is the inverse — strong digital presence, modern treatment menu, contemporary positioning. column25: GMC-registered GP with 13 years clinical experience and ongoing NHS practice. column5: Anaphylaxis kit (adrenaline 1:1000, antihistamine), oxygen if applicable, and basic resuscitation equipment.

column4: ICO data controller registration active
column5: ICO registration must be active before processing any patient data. £47/year.

column5: Safeguarding adults and children at risk — referral pathways, designated lead, training requirements.

column5: Patient complaints procedure, response timelines, investigation process and learning outcomes.

column18: Contractor verifies ceiling void, existing electrics, drainage routes, ventilation, fire alarm, emergency lighting and access.

column4: Formal landlord consent for proposed internal alterations including partitioning, plumbing, electrical works, vinyl flooring and fire doors
column18: Covers landlord consent support for partitions, basins, electrics, signage fixings, shopfront repaint and any penetrations.

column4: Receive full handover pack including all certificates, warranties, O&M manuals, as-built drawings and sign-off documentation
column18: Electrical certs, plumbing details, fire door info, warranties, as-builts, flooring specs, paint specs and handover evidence.

column4: Opening retail stock of Obagi and other skincare products for clinic retail area
column18: Lean opening retail stock. Extra retail stock is a stretch/deferred item.

column18: Final payment should not be released until critical snags and certificates are resolved.

column3: Professional Deep Clean Before Clinical Setup
column4: Professional deep clean of all surfaces before clinical furniture and equipment is installed
column18: Post-works clean before stock and full clinical setup.
Final go/no-go before opening.

column3: Contractor Pre-Start Meeting and Programme Confirmation
column4: Pre-start meeting with principal contractor to confirm programme, sequencing, responsibilities, site rules and communication protocols
column18: Confirm programme, access, responsibilities, certificates, inspection points and handover requirements.

column4: Incremental electrical works only — new dedicated circuits to 2 treatment rooms, minimum 4 double sockets per room, clinical lamp positions, emergency lighting check and additions, EICR on completion
column18: Treatment room sockets, lighting changes, emergency lighting implications, certification. If included in contractor package later, set this line to included inside another package.

column4: Supply and installation of medical-grade impervious sheet vinyl (Polyflor Expona or equivalent) in treatment rooms only. Hot-welded seams throughout, fully coved 100mm upstand at all skirting junctions
column18: Clinical vinyl to treatment areas only unless final quote expands scope.

column4: Reception desk area furniture, waiting chairs, side tables, accessories and styling elements
column18: Lean premium reception styling using off-the-shelf items.

column3: Furniture / Equipment Installation and Room Dressing
column4: Final installation of all furniture and equipment, room dressing and styling to opening standard
column18: Installation/setup task only. Costs captured in FF&E lines.

column4: Electric/motorised clinical treatment couch for primary treatment room
column18: Couch only. Does not include full treatment room equipment.

column4: Document adverse event protocol including escalation pathway, emergency contacts and reporting requirements
column18: Vascular occlusion, anaphylaxis, vasovagal and emergency response procedure.

column18: Gloves, gauze, couch roll, disinfectant, sharps containers, disposables, aftercare materials.

Keep existing accounting system live and ready for Winchester cost tracking.

column4: Medical indemnity, public liability and premises insurance for Winchester clinic

column4: Obtain final Building Control completion certificate confirming all works are compliant
column18: Completion sign-off. Cost captured in Building Control line.

column4: Repaint shopfront exterior from existing purple/dark colour to off-white. Includes all external woodwork, frames and plinth

column3: Joinery / Reception Desk / Clinical Cabinetry — Installation

column4: clinic_open_time
column18: max_clinic_days_per_week

column6: First client at 9:45 after drop-off. Proper 30-minute lunch — no phone, no consultations. Last client finishes by 17:15, out the door by 17:30. Thursday is a short day — treat it as a half day, don't try to cram it. No admin during client hours; that's for the commute or Monday.
column17: My contract requires 4 weeks' notice. The surgery may try to negotiate a longer handover. I'll offer 6 weeks? as a goodwill gesture but I won't extend beyond that. I want to leave on good terms; I may want a reference and I'll need NMC registration maintained. David knows the date. It is not negotiable.
column19: Cancellations go out via ANS as early as possible with a personal message from Abi. Clients are prioritised for rescheduling before the general diary opens. A buffer of savings is maintained specifically to absorb a week of zero income without panic. No guilt, one sick day handled well protects the 290+ review record.
column20: Two weeks clinic closure per year minimum - one in summer, one at Christmas. Dates blocked in ANS before the diary opens for that period. Clients notified 6 weeks in advance. No bookings accepted for those periods. David is aware these are non-negotiable before the lease is signed.
column22:
Building something that is entirely mine and Abi's together. 
Seeing clients and genuinely changing how they feel. 
Having a diary I control - no shifts, no rota, no one deciding my hours. Being there when the kids get home. 
Watching something we built from nothing become something real.
column24:
David + family and friends - practical and emotional. 
David + family and friends - school logistics. 
Sarah Hartfree / Arron - professional peer.
TBD: a business mentor or aesthetics practitioner peer group. 
ACE membership should help with the latter - worth activating.

I offer personalised treatments to help people look and feel their best.","successVision12m":"The diary books 3 weeks out without me doing anything active. 
I'm doing five clinic days — Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday and Saturday. 
Sunday and Monday are mine. I'm home by 5:30 and actually present when I get there. Elsy and Eli know the routine. 
David does two school runs a week and it's just normal now, not a favour. I've cleared my nursing income. 
I've taken a proper holiday with my phone off. 
When someone asks what I do, I say I own a clinic and I don't follow it up with a qualification.
When I am not in office, I set an out of office and respond during clinical hours. 
I feel like myself. Not the nurse who left. column4: title
Soft launch date set (invite-only — friends, family & Bedhampton regulars)
column7: 📅 Target: 25 October 2026. Block out 25–29 October for a soft launch; invitations to go to close friends, family, loyal Bedhampton regulars, and key local contacts (e.g., Winchester BID members). Use Paperless Post or a simple email invitation (bcc) to share RSVP details. Clearly state that this is an invite-only preview before the grand opening. Confirm your invitee list and send invites by mid-September, follow up a week before the event.

column4: Waitlist email campaign drafted — opening announcement + direct booking link
column7: 📅 Target: 4 October 2026. Draft your waitlist email campaign — subject line suggestions: 'Be First: Book Your Welcome Appointment at Abi Peters Aesthetics' or 'Secure Your Spot at Winchester’s Newest Aesthetic Clinic.' Structure: warm welcome, short intro to Abi/USP, opening date, offer details, high-impact photo, and a bold CTA button to book (link to Fresha). End with benefits of booking early and easy contact info.

column4: Launch week: Grand opening post, story series, first booking celebrations
column7: 📅 Target: 1 November 2026. Mark launch week with a festive Reel of the first clients arriving (with their written permission), ribbon cutting, or local influencer drop-ins. Share a 'thank you' static post—Abi on the clinic steps, flowers in hand, or with the team. Story series: reactions, team intros, and first booking celebrations. Keep captions heartfelt: 'Today, our doors open—thank you Winchester, for welcoming your new clinic.' Cross-post highlights to GBP and invite Google reviews from early clients.

column4: Instagram — link in bio set (booking URL or landing page)
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026 — Add your Fresha booking URL or your website’s booking landing page as the clickable link in your Instagram bio. Use a service like Linktree or bio.fm only if you need multiple links; otherwise, keep it as the direct booking link for simplicity and conversion.

column4: Google Business Profile — created
column7: 📅 Target: 23 August 2026 — Sign up at https://business.google.com/ using Abi Peters Aesthetics as the business name, set the category to 'Medical Spa' or 'Skin Care Clinic', and use the address 9A Jewry Street, Winchester, Hampshire SO23 8RZ. Enter the suggested phone number and opening hours (Tue–Sat 9am–5pm). Complete all required fields and confirm the map pin location. Submit for verification immediately to allow time for the mailed postcard process.

column4: Brand fonts selected and licensed
column7: 📅 Target: 26 July 2026 — Select 1–2 premium, readable typefaces (e.g., 'Playfair Display' for headers, 'Montserrat' or 'Lato' for body). Check Google Fonts/Fontspring for commercial-use options and purchase or license as necessary. Confirm web and print compatibility with your designer. Set up a master brand guidelines doc noting all font uses.

column4: Photography session — completed
column7: 📅 Target: 9 August 2026 — Complete the session with all brand assets and locations ready. Shoot a mix of portrait, candid, and interiors. Dress in your nurse uniform, bring branded props if available. Request at least 10 edited hero shots plus social-ready crops for your website and Instagram. Confirm usage rights for marketing and web.

column4: Consent form templates ready
column7: 📅 Target: 9 August 2026 — Adapt consent form templates from JCCP guidance and Insurer (Hamilton Fraser) example forms. Tailor each to Botox, fillers, and advanced skin treatments. Ensure they’re compliant, require signature/date, and include APA branding and 9A Jewry St address. Save print-ready and editable Word/PDF files.

column4: ⚠ Compliance reminder: no before/after client photos without separate written consent
Google Ads campaigns set to go-live on opening date
column7: 📅 Target: 4 October 2026. Draft Google Ads campaigns (use Smart Campaign for 'aesthetic clinic in Winchester', 'nurse-led aesthetics Hampshire', etc.) including copy, location targeting, keywords, ad assets, and budget (£15–£30/day for pre-launch/test). Set campaigns to go live on 1 November in line with opening. Install tracking on your website/Fresha so bookings from Ads are traceable.

column4: Week –10: Treatment education series begins (one treatment per post)
column7: 📅 Target: 23 August 2026. Begin an educational Reel or carousel series focusing on one treatment per week (e.g., anti-wrinkle, dermal fillers, skin boosters). Use accessible visuals: e.g., you speaking to camera in-clinic, animated overlays, or props—not before/afters unless written consent. Each post should answer: what is this treatment, who is it for, what’s unique about Abi Peters Aesthetics, and expected outcomes (without guaranteeing). Caption hook: 'Ever wondered what [treatment] can do for you? Here’s the honest nurse-led lowdown.' End with a gentle CTA to DM questions. Hashtags: #WinchesterBeauty #MedicalAesthetics.

column4: Instagram — 9-post grid planned and ready for launch day
column7: 📅 Target: 9 August 2026 — Plan a 9-post launch grid in Canva: 1. Brand/logo reveal, 2. Abi’s intro, 3. Opening date, 4–6. Signature treatments, 7. Welcome offer teaser, 8. Clinic location reveal, 9. 'Book now' post. Schedule posts in Meta Business Suite or Later for a cohesive launch week.

column4: Treatment menu finalised
column7: 📅 Target: 9 August 2026 — List all treatments offered at launch: injectables, advanced skin procedures, skincare consults, product retail. Create descriptions (1–2 sentences, accessible yet clinical) for each, emphasizing nurse-led expertise. Organize treatments by category and order of popularity. Get input from a trusted peer or local nurse if possible.

column4: Brand colour palette locked
column7: 📅 Target: 26 July 2026 — Lock in a primary palette using 2-3 sophisticated neutrals with one signature accent (e.g. soft gold, slate blue, or sage green). Use Coolors or Adobe Colour to experiment, or request the designer provide swatches for digital and print (CMYK & HEX). Keep the finish clinical but inviting — colours should evoke trust and luxury, not clinical coldness.

column4: Local micro-influencers identified (3–5 in Hampshire aesthetic space)
column7: 📅 Target: 4 October 2026. On Instagram, search #WinchesterBeauty and #HampshireLifestyle for accounts in aesthetics/beauty/wellness with 2–10k followers. Vet engagement (avg. 100+ likes/reel or positive comments), quality of content, and tone. Add leads to a shortlist of 5–10; aim to select 3–5 for opening campaign. Prepare a short pitch introducing Abi Peters Aesthetics and the opportunity for a gifted treatment experience share.

column4: Week –8: Behind the scenes — fit-out, build & clinic taking shape
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026. Share behind-the-scenes Reels and Stories of the fit-out progress—unboxing, paint choices, key milestones, or a quick walkthrough of the space at 9A Jewry Street. Collaborate with local suppliers (e.g., shoutout @WessexInteriors or decorators if used) for cross-promo. Caption hook: 'Your new clinic is coming to life—here's a peek inside!' Close each post with inviting community responses: 'What would you like to see in our lounge?'. Use photo, time-lapse, or montage content. Hashtags: #WinchesterAesthetics #ClinicBuild #LocalBusiness.

column4: Facebook — business page created and optimised
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026 — Create a Facebook business page for Abi Peters Aesthetics. Upload the logo and cover photo, add address and opening hours, and fill the 'About' section detailing 'Premium nurse-led aesthetics in Winchester.' Sync your Instagram and add the Fresha booking button. Invite friends and previous clients to like the page.

column4: Price list finalised and designed
column7: 📅 Target: 9 August 2026 — Review Winchester competitor pricing for each treatment (especially injectables/skin). Finalise APA’s menu with clear, premium pricing, e.g., ‘Botox from £190/area, Dermal fillers from £295/ml’. Design a one-page PDF price list (Canva or via your designer) in your brand style, ready for print and web. Proofread thoroughly and save as PDF.

column4: Clinic name & domain name confirmed
column7: 📅 Target: 12 July 2026 — Officially register 'Abi Peters Aesthetics' as your clinic name and secure your domain (strongly suggest abiapetersaesthetics.co.uk or apaesthetics.co.uk for clarity and SEO). Use Google Domains or 123-Reg to purchase ASAP. Also reserve matching Instagram handle (@abiapetersaesthetics or @apaestheticswinchester if available).

column4: Influencer gifted treatment arranged and pre-launch content agreed
column7: 📅 Target: 20 September 2026. Contact top local micro-influencers and offer a complimentary treatment in exchange for an honest Instagram post or Reel about their Abi Peters Aesthetics experience — clarify no obligation for a positive review, just a genuine share. Get agreement in writing (email or Docusign) outlining the treatment, posting expectations (media, timings, tags), and consent for content re-share on your channels.

column4: Week –12: Brand reveal teaser + introducing the practitioner
column7: 📅 Target: 9 August 2026. Start your content calendar with a suspenseful 'coming soon' teaser, using a 5–7 second Reel with a blurred logo, hints of your signature colour palette, and Winchester backdrops (e.g., Cathedral Close, the High Street). Caption hook: 'Something new is arriving in Winchester... Can you guess what?' Then, in a follow-up static post or carousel, introduce yourself (Abi Peters) as a nurse-led aesthetics expert—share your story and local connection. Highlight credentials but keep tone warm and friendly. Hashtags: #WinchesterAesthetics #AbiPetersAesthetics #MeetAbi #NurseLed. Tag @WinchesterBid for local reach.

column4: Google Business Profile — opening hours, photos & first post live
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026 — Add opening hours (Tue–Sat 9am–5pm), practice photos (exterior, treatment room, headshot of Abi), and publish an introduction post ('Excited to open Abi Peters Aesthetics in Winchester – bookings now available!'). Use professional launch-day photos and ensure the Fresha booking link is visible under 'Appointments'.

column4: Photography session — booked
column7: 📅 Target: 9 August 2026 — Book a Winchester-based photographer who specializes in lifestyle/branding (e.g., search on Instagram, Bark, or ask Winchester BID for recommendations). Specify shots needed: you in clinic uniform, treatment room at 9A Jewry St, local city-centre backdrops, hero skincare products. Request a mix of lifestyle and clinical feel, with natural light. Transfer deposit to confirm date.

column4: Photographer / videographer booked for opening day
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026. Search the Hampshire Wedding & Portrait Photographers Association for photographers (e.g., www.hampshireweddingphotographers.com). Contact 2–3 for day rates (£400–£600) and availability for 1 November. Share a written creative brief covering clean clinical shots, customer journey images, treatment demo, group shots, and Abi’s headshots. Request copyright use for website and social.

column4: Week –6: Social proof — testimonials from Bedhampton clients (consent required)
column7: 📅 Target: 20 September 2026. Gather Bedhampton client testimonials (secure written consent first; reach out via text/DM or a consent form from your booking system) and share as carousels or Reels, overlaying their feedback on cheerful local photos or short video clips. Emphasize trust and nurse expertise, using captions like: 'Why do clients travel for Abi Peters Aesthetics? Hear in their own words.' Tag the location as 'Winchester' and highlight the move. Reminder: Never use direct before/after unless separate documented consent is received. Hashtags: #ClientLove #HampshireAesthetics.

column4: Fresha listing — clinic profile created
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026 — Sign up for Fresha (https://partners.fresha.com/) and complete the clinic profile: use the business name, logo, and full address. Write an engaging description highlighting Abi’s qualifications and the clinic’s unique nurse-led approach. Upload high-quality profile and header photos.

column4: Mailchimp — welcome email sequence drafted (minimum 3 emails)
column7: 📅 Target: 20 September 2026 — In Mailchimp, draft a three-part welcome sequence: 1) Welcome + 'Meet Abi' (include credentials, approach); 2) 'What to expect at your first appointment' (arrival, consultation, aftercare); 3) Exclusive opening offer reveal with a Fresha booking link. Test delivery and personalisation for new sign-ups.

column4: Friends & family preview event run
column7: 📅 Target: 25–29 October 2026. Run your friends & family preview event during soft launch week. Confirm appointment slots, set up the treatment room for professional photos, and gather informal testimonials. Encourage F&F to post on Instagram (tagging your account) for early social proof. Offer light refreshments or a small thank-you gift for participants.

column4: Week –2: Countdown content — 14 days to go, final previews & anticipation
column7: 📅 Target: 18 October 2026. Start a daily/alternate day countdown on Stories, showing the final touches—welcome packs being prepped, product stock arriving, Abi reviewing protocols, spotlights on your favourite local Hampshire businesses. Reels/Stories: '14 days to go', building to '1 day to go!' Suggest an engagement Story (e.g., poll: 'Who’ll be popping into 9A Jewry St?'). Create buzz but keep tone friendly, not pushy. Hashtags: #WinchesterCountdown #AbiPetersAesthetics.

column4: Website — SEO title, meta description & Google Search Console set up
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026 — Set SEO title: 'Abi Peters Aesthetics | Nurse-Led Aesthetics Clinic Winchester'. Meta description: 'Expert medical aesthetics, botox, and skin treatments at Abi Peters Aesthetics, Winchester. Book your consultation today.' Add your site to Google Search Console at launch and submit your sitemap for indexing.

column4: Mailchimp / email list — account set up & sign-up form live
column7: 📅 Target: 20 September 2026 — Create a Mailchimp account and embed the sign-up form on your website and as a booking form opt-in. Collect email addresses via website, socials, and in-person waitlist. Set up list segmentation ready for marketing compliance (GDPR).

column4: Google Ads — account created, search campaign drafted
column7: 📅 Target: 4 October 2026 — Set up a Google Ads account and draft a Search campaign targeting Winchester + 15-mile radius, women 28–55 with interests in beauty, skincare, wellness. Use keywords: 'aesthetics clinic Winchester', 'botox Winchester', 'lip filler Winchester Hampshire'. Write ad copy like 'Opening Soon – Abi Peters Aesthetics. Book advanced skincare & injectables with an expert nurse in Winchester.'

column4: Instagram — handle, bio & highlight covers set
column7: 📅 Target: 23 August 2026 — Secure @abiapetersaesthetics on Instagram (or fallback: @abi.peters.aesthetics if unavailable). Set the bio to: 'Premium nurse-led aesthetics clinic in Winchester. Advanced injectables, skin & wellness. Book now ▼'. Create custom highlight covers in brand colours for 'Treatments', 'About Abi', 'Offers', and 'FAQs.'

column4: Website / landing page — live with custom domain
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026 — Register abiapetersaesthetics.co.uk on Squarespace, Wix, or WordPress (based on preferred ease of use). Build a one-page site with sections for bio, services, location, and contact; embed the Fresha booking widget for instant online appointments. Go live with a mobile-optimised layout and all core info.

column4: Business cards & stationery ordered
column7: 📅 Target: 9 August 2026 — Once logo and colours are ready, design and order premium business cards and starter stationery pack (letterheads, gift cards) from Moo, Vistaprint, or a Winchester print shop. Ensure all contain logo, clinic details, website, and social handles. Order a small batch (<250) for soft launch and VIP invitations.

column4: Grand opening date confirmed and announced publicly
column7: 📅 Target: 1 November 2026. Confirm the grand opening for Sunday, 1 November 2026, and prepare a public announcement for all platforms (website, Instagram, email, Google Business Profile). Suggested wording: 'We’re opening our doors! Join us on 1 November at Abi Peters Aesthetics, 9A Jewry Street, for our grand opening.' Schedule announcement posts for 4 October and a countdown reminder during opening week.

column4: PR press release drafted and sent to local Hampshire press
column7: 📅 Target: 4 October 2026. Draft press release and send to Hampshire Chronicle (news@hampshirechronicle.co.uk), Winchester BID (info@winchesterbid.co.uk), So Hampshire Magazine (editorial@sohampshire.co.uk), and About My Area Winchester. Highlight 'nurse-led', new local business, introductory offer, and opening date. Include 1–2 professional photos, website/socials links, and Abi’s contact for interviews. Follow up by 11 October.

column4: Post-launch review booked (2 weeks after opening — what worked, what to double down on)
column7: 📅 Target: 15 November 2026. Schedule a post-launch review with your marketing consultant or team (if any). Prepare to discuss metrics: bookings, offer uptake, social reach, feedback from clients/influencers, Google Ads results, and what worked in PR/influencer outreach. Identify what to double down on for month two, any process tweaks, and opportunities for ongoing word-of-mouth.

column4: Launch week content pre-scheduled (all posts loaded in advance)
column7: 📅 Target: 18 October 2026. Prepare and pre-schedule all launch week social posts (Instagram, Facebook, Google Business Profile, email)—including grand opening countdown, behind-the-scenes setup, team/practitioner intros, opening offer reminders, and FAQ Reels. Use Meta Business Suite or Later.com for advance scheduling. Double-check copy accuracy, image rights, and correct tags.

column4: Opening offer confirmed (e.g. 20% off first booking, limited to first 30 clients)
column7: 📅 Target: 4 October 2026. Confirm and finalize your opening offer: 'Introductory Offer — £150 off your first treatment package (minimum spend £350), available to the first 30 clients only. Offer closes 30 November 2026.' Update all promos, website and pre-schedule this for the launch announcement. Prepare offer terms and T&Cs for website clarity.

column4: Week –4: Pre-launch waitlist CTA + opening date announcement
column7: 📅 Target: 4 October 2026. Announce the official opening date with a bright Reel or carousel—show the clinic exterior, a special welcome message, or a map graphic of 9A Jewry Street for local relevance. Launch your pre-booking waitlist via a simple Typeform or Fresha link in your bio and make the CTA clear ('Book your place now—spaces are filling fast!'). Capture urgency but avoid 'guaranteed results' language. Tag Winchester accounts for local reach. Caption idea: 'Be the first in Winchester to experience the future of aesthetics—book your priority opening spot today.'

column4: Google Business Profile — verified (allow 5–14 days)
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026 — Once Google mails your verification code, input it promptly (should arrive within 5–14 days after profile creation). Monitor email and physical mail for Google’s postcard with the code. Mark the listing as officially open from the planned opening day (1 November 2026) in the profile settings.

column4: Fresha listing — services, prices & online booking enabled
column7: 📅 Target: 6 September 2026 — Enter a full list of signature treatments (e.g., botox, lip filler, skin boosters), set transparent pricing, and switch on instant online booking. Integrate your Instagram for credibility and ensure the Fresha page links to your website. Triple-check opening hours and test the booking flow as a new client.

column4: Canva Pro / design tool — brand kit set up with logo, colours & fonts
column7: 📅 Target: 26 July 2026 — Once brand colours and fonts are final, set up your Canva Pro Brand Kit with the logo (SVG/PNG from the designer), all approved typefaces, and brand colour palette as soon as they are provided. Create quick-access templates for social posts, stories, and highlight icons for consistent branding from the outset.

column4: Logo finalised (JPEG, PNG & SVG versions)
column7: 📅 Target: 9 August 2026 — Commission a professional logo designer (e.g., Looka, 99designs, or a Winchester-based freelancer) for the final logo, ensuring a mix of clinical elegance and local warmth. Request versions in JPEG, PNG (transparent), and SVG formats. Provide the designer with brand inspiration (Trinny London, Medik8) and a mood board for orientation. Approve the final design and save all asset files securely in Google Drive or your preferred cloud storage.

{"parts": {"1": "The Bedhampton revenue figure of £10,000 per month is the single most critical load-bearing assumption in this model, yet it is treated as a static constant throughout the Winchester ramp period. Should Bedhampton underperform by 20% during the first six months of Winchester operations — dropping from £10,000 to £8,000 gross, reducing net profit from £7,400 to £5,520 per month — the model's entire cash position collapses. In the realistic ramp scenario, Month 1 shows Winchester generating a net loss of £2,508 against Bedhampton's £7,400 profit, yielding a combined net of £4,892. A 20% Bedhampton shortfall transforms that £4,892 into £3,012. By Month 3, when Winchester is supposed to cross into positive territory at £398 net profit, the combined position under a Bedhampton shortfall scenario drops to £5,918 instead of £7,798 — a cumulative six-month cash variance exceeding £11,000. This matters because the model has no mechanism for Bedhampton revenue protection during the period when founder attention shifts to Winchester launch marketing, fit-out supervision, and operational establishment. The implicit assumption that Bedhampton runs itself at constant output while the founder focuses elsewhere is operationally naive.

The pre-opening cost structure contains a timing trap that has not been modelled. The lease documentation indicates two months of rent before opening at £2,708 per month, offset by three rent-free months from the landlord, producing what the model describes as a pre-opening property cash cost of negative £2,708. This calculation assumes construction and fit-out complete within the rent-free window. A three-month construction delay — entirely plausible given the model shows only 2 of 112 launch tasks complete with 7 in progress — would eliminate the rent-free benefit entirely and add £8,124 in rent costs before a single client walks through the door. The launch budget already shows a £105,457 variance gap between the selected £81,786 and the high estimate of £187,243. A construction delay does not merely add rent; it compounds into extended insurance premiums, extended utility standing charges, and the carrying cost of stock already purchased for an opening that has not occurred. The model's pre-opening figure of £81,786 assumes on-time delivery. The high estimate of £187,243 presumably captures some delay risk, but nowhere does the model show how a delay interacts with the staged rent structure or when the rent-free clock begins ticking.

The closure trigger for Bedhampton — defined as Winchester net margin reaching 20% of gross revenue — creates a timing gap that the model does not address. Winchester's realistic scenario shows net margin of 19% at 70% occupancy, generating £4,757 net profit on £24,794 gross revenue. The 20% threshold implies Winchester gross revenue of approximately £23,835 with a net margin of £4,767. Under the realistic ramp, Winchester crosses the self-funding threshold somewhere between Month 5 and Month 6. The model assumes Bedhampton continues generating £7,400 per month until closure, then stops. But the period between Winchester reaching marginal viability and Bedhampton actually closing creates a window where both clinics operate at the lower bound of acceptable economics simultaneously. If Winchester reaches 19% margin in Month 6 but the closure trigger demands 20%, Bedhampton continues. The founder now operates two locations — one generating £4,757 monthly profit, one generating £7,400 monthly profit — but with no capacity buffer for client overflow, no contingency for equipment failure at either site, and no labour flexibility since the model shows £0 staffing costs at both locations, meaning the founder is the sole practitioner.

The fixed cost schedule for Winchester is missing entire categories that will materialise in operation. Equipment maintenance contracts for laser and IPL devices, injectable refrigeration units, and treatment beds are not line items. Consumables inflation is absent — the model applies a static 25.3% stock-to-revenue ratio, but pharmaceutical and cosmeceutical supply chains have experienced 8-15% annual price increases since 2022. Staff illness cover shows as £0 because staffing shows as £0, but if the founder falls ill, Winchester generates zero revenue against £8,805 in fixed costs — a position that burns £8,805 per month with no income offset except the £4,500 nursing income, which itself disappears if the illness affects the founder's nursing work simultaneously. Regulatory inspection costs from the CQC or local authority are not budgeted. Insurance premiums are modelled at £158 per month for clinical, premises and liability cover, plus £40 for employers liability, but these figures represent Year 1 quotes. Post-launch claims history, even minor incident reports, can escalate premiums by 25-40% at renewal. The model shows no contingency for this.

The owner drawings figure of £0 per month is presented alongside a target drawings at maturity of £4,166 per month. The personal salary need is modelled at £0. School fees are £0. Travel costs are £0. Other household costs are £0. This creates an implausible picture of a founder with zero personal financial obligations beyond what nursing income covers. The £4,500 nursing income plus £32,500 runway savings — described as providing 99 months of cash runway — assumes the founder draws nothing from the business during ramp and maintains zero personal expenditure beyond nursing coverage. If actual household costs are £2,000 per month (a conservative figure for a professional with clinical registration fees, professional development requirements, and vehicle costs for multi-site operation), the nursing income surplus drops to £2,500 per month. Under the conservative ramp scenario, Winchester loses £4,678 in Month 1, £4,446 in Month 2, and continues losing approximately £3,000-4,000 per month through Month 12. If Bedhampton's £7,400 profit covers Winchester's losses, the combined net of £2,722 in Month 1 under conservative assumptions barely exceeds the unstated household costs. The £32,500 runway, divided by a realistic £2,000 monthly personal draw, provides 16 months — not 99 months. The model's runway calculation appears to assume zero personal costs, which is not how human beings operate.

The model contains no provision for the cost of client acquisition failure recovery. If Winchester opens in November 2026 and achieves only 21% occupancy in Month 1 (£7,544 gross, £4,678 net loss per the conservative scenario), the £600 monthly marketing spend is fixed. Increasing marketing to accelerate client acquisition requires additional capital the model does not allocate. The £32,500 runway is positioned as buffer capital, but if that runway must fund both lifestyle costs and marketing escalation simultaneously, it depletes at triple the modelled rate. The assumption that £600 per month in advertising — shared across two clinics — can drive Winchester from cold start to 70% occupancy in six months has no supporting evidence in this model. Client acquisition cost is not calculated. Lifetime value is not calculated. The 60% repeat booking rate is asserted but not substantiated against Bedhampton historical data. If repeat booking runs at 40% rather than 60%, the model's occupancy trajectory requires 50% more new client acquisition per month to maintain the same revenue curve — acquisition the fixed £600 marketing budget cannot deliver.", "2": "The first tripwire in this model is not revenue — it is the ratio of new client bookings to total slots filled at Winchester. The model assumes a 60% repeat booking rate, which means 40% of monthly appointments must come from new client acquisition. At Month 1 under the realistic scenario, 32.5% occupancy translates to 50 slots filled; 20 of those must be new clients. If new client bookings fall below 15 in any of the first three months, the repeat flywheel cannot mathematically generate the 7.5 percentage point monthly occupancy increase baked into the realistic ramp. This tripwire gives approximately 60 days of lead time before the occupancy shortfall becomes visible in revenue figures, because a new client acquired today does not generate their repeat booking for 6-8 weeks. By the time Month 3 revenue comes in below the projected £16,824, the acquisition failure occurred in Month 1 and the intervention window has already closed.

The second tripwire is Winchester stock cost as a percentage of Winchester revenue. The model assumes 25.3% consistently, yielding £2,912 in variable costs at Month 1's £11,512 revenue. If stock percentage exceeds 28% in any month during the first quarter, that is not a supplier pricing issue — it is evidence of treatment mix skewing toward lower-margin procedures or, worse, discounting to fill slots. At 28% stock cost with Month 2's projected £14,168 revenue, variable costs rise from £3,585 to £3,967, eroding £382 from an already thin £-1,055 net position. This tripwire has only 30 days of lead time because stock is purchased in advance of treatments delivered; by the time the margin compression appears in monthly accounts, the pricing or mix problem is already embedded in client expectations and reversing it requires either losing clients or accepting confrontation. Two consecutive months above 27% stock percentage should trigger immediate treatment menu restructuring.

The third tripwire is the relationship between Winchester marketing spend and new client conversion. The model shows £600/month in shared advertising plus £82/month for GoHighLevel, allocated across both sites. Winchester's share is unstated but presumably half, meaning approximately £341/month in attributable marketing cost. At Month 1's required 20 new clients, that implies a customer acquisition cost ceiling of £17 per new client to stay within budget. If CAC exceeds £25 in Month 1 — which is only 47% above target — then achieving the 50 new clients needed over the first three months requires either doubling marketing spend to £682/month or accepting that occupancy will plateau below break-even. This tripwire has 45 days of lead time: advertising campaigns launched in Week 1 of Month 1 will show conversion data by Week 3, giving two weeks to adjust before Month 2 spend is committed. However, the £600 monthly budget is fixed in the model with no contingency allocation, so the adjustment mechanism is unclear.

The fourth tripwire is Bedhampton revenue stability. The entire model depends on Bedhampton contributing £7,400/month in net profit to offset Winchester's losses during ramp. Bedhampton currently generates £10,000 gross with 26% stock cost and zero fixed costs — an unusually favourable structure that suggests either understated costs or a rent-free arrangement that may not persist. If Bedhampton gross revenue falls below £8,500 in any month, the combined operation's cushion compresses from £7,400 to £6,290 net, which under the conservative scenario would push combined monthly profit from £2,722 to £1,612 in Month 1. This tripwire has the longest lead time — approximately 90 days — because Bedhampton's client base is established and decline would manifest as gradual attrition rather than sudden collapse. However, it also has the least controllability: if Bedhampton clients are migrating to Winchester rather than adding to total demand, the owner faces a cannibalisation problem with no good solution.

The fifth tripwire is the gap between Winchester fixed costs and the cumulative P&L trajectory. Fixed costs are £8,805/month regardless of revenue. Under the conservative scenario, cumulative P&L at Month 6 is £19,935 — meaning the business has absorbed six months of £8,805 fixed costs (£52,830) plus variable costs while generating only enough gross revenue to show £19,935 cumulative profit. If Month 4 cumulative P&L falls below £10,000, the business is tracking worse than conservative and the £32,500 runway savings — which the model claims provides 99 months of coverage — becomes relevant far sooner than projected. The 99-month runway figure is calculated against some unstated burn rate; at a combined net of £2,722/month under conservative Month 1, savings would extend runway by only 12 months, not 99. This discrepancy suggests the runway calculation assumes steady-state profitability, not ramp-phase losses, and should be treated as unreliable.

The interaction effects are where this model breaks catastrophically. If tripwire one (new client acquisition below 15/month) fires simultaneously with tripwire three (CAC above £25), the plan is not stressed — it is structurally broken. Low acquisition at high cost means the marketing channel is failing, and increasing spend produces negative returns. Under this scenario, Month 3 occupancy stalls at approximately 35% rather than reaching 47.5%, Winchester net loss deepens to approximately £-3,400/month rather than the projected £398 profit, and combined net drops to £4,000/month. Cumulative P&L at Month 6 would be approximately £16,000 rather than £19,935 under conservative or £51,147 under realistic. The business would consume runway savings to remain solvent while having no mechanism to accelerate growth. If tripwire two (stock percentage above 28%) fires simultaneously with tripwire four (Bedhampton revenue below £8,500), the margin structure of both sites is failing concurrently, which suggests either market pricing pressure or operational cost creep that is systemic rather than site-specific. This combination indicates the owner's cost management capabilities are insufficient for a two-site operation and no amount of occupancy improvement will restore profitability. These two simultaneous tripwires mean exit or recapitalisation, not adjustment.", "3": "The month-end report shows Winchester at 21% occupancy, generating £7,544 in gross revenue against £8,805 in fixed costs before a single syringe has been opened. The clinic is burning £4,678 per month before Bedhampton's £7,400 contribution, leaving a combined net of just £2,722. The founder has 48 hours to decide whether this is a slow start that corrects itself or the beginning of a death spiral. The playbook that follows assumes she chooses to fight.

The revenue response begins with the £600 monthly marketing allocation, which is currently modelled as a shared dual-clinic cost with no channel specificity. Winchester's SO23 postcode sits in one of the most affluent local authority areas in Hampshire, with median household incomes approximately 40% above the national average and a female population aged 35-55 that over-indexes on premium service consumption. The immediate action is to reallocate the entire £600 budget to a single acquisition channel — Meta advertising targeting women aged 30-55 within a 12-mile radius of Winchester city centre, with household income targeting enabled and interest categories limited to skincare, cosmetic procedures, and premium beauty brands. The offer structure must account for aesthetics booking psychology: unlike hair or nails, injectable and skin treatments require prospect education and trust-building that typically spans 4-8 weeks from first ad impression to booked consultation. A £600 monthly spend at Winchester-appropriate cost-per-click rates of £1.80-2.40 will generate approximately 250-330 clicks monthly, converting at 3-5% to enquiries yields 8-16 leads, of which 40-60% will book consultations within 6 weeks, and 70% of consultations convert to treatment. This arithmetic produces 2-7 new clients per month from paid acquisition alone, representing £460-1,610 in monthly revenue materialising in weeks 6-10, not week 2. The founder must understand that marketing action taken on day one of month two will not appear in revenue until mid-month three at earliest, creating a mandatory cash absorption period that cannot be compressed regardless of spend level.

The second revenue lever is the 60% repeat booking rate, which at 21% occupancy in month one means approximately 28 treatments delivered, of which 17 should have rebooked. If rebooking is falling below this threshold, the immediate operational response is mandatory rebooking before the client leaves the treatment room, with the next appointment entered into the system before payment is processed. Every client who leaves without a future booking must trigger same-day follow-up via the GoHighLevel automation — the £82 monthly cost for this platform is only justified if rebooking sequences are built and active. If they are not built by month one, that is an operational failure that must be corrected within 72 hours, not at the next convenient planning session.

The cost response is constrained by the structure of the fixed cost base. The £2,708 monthly rent and £1,300 business rates represent £4,008 in property costs that are contractually fixed for the lease term — these cannot be touched. The £158 Hamilton Fraser clinical insurance and £40 employers liability insurance totalling £198 monthly are legally and professionally required — these cannot be touched. The £180 electricity, £30 water, and £40 internet totalling £250 in utilities have minimal discretionary component when a clinical space must maintain heating, lighting, and connectivity. The professional registrations — £10 NMC, £15 RCN, £20 ACE, £40 JCCP/BCAM totalling £85 monthly — are non-negotiable for a regulated practitioner. This means £4,541 of the £8,805 monthly fixed cost base, representing 52%, is immovable in month one regardless of revenue performance.

The costs that can theoretically be cut require lead time. The £120 monthly accountant cost could be reduced by moving to quarterly rather than monthly bookkeeping, but this requires 30-60 days notice depending on the engagement letter and is inadvisable during a cash crisis when financial visibility matters most. The £47 clinical waste collection could potentially be reduced to fortnightly rather than weekly collection, saving perhaps £20-25 monthly, but this requires renegotiation and may breach clinical hygiene requirements depending on treatment volume. The £600 marketing spend could be eliminated entirely to save cash, but this is the equivalent of cutting off oxygen to treat a fever — the patient stops feeling unwell because the patient is dead.

The only genuine month-one cost reduction available without contractual or operational consequence is the aggressive renegotiation of stock costs. At 25.3% of revenue, the £1,909 variable cost at conservative occupancy represents the second-largest expense category after rent. The immediate action is to contact every supplier and request 60-day payment terms rather than 30-day, followed by a systematic review of whether own-brand alternatives exist for high-volume consumables. A 3% reduction in stock percentage, achievable through supplier negotiation and product rationalisation, would save £227 monthly at conservative occupancy and £753 monthly at realistic occupancy — meaningful but not transformative.

The personal finance response is where the model's peculiar structure becomes operationally relevant. The £4,500 monthly nursing income is currently the primary personal cash flow, with £0 modelled for drawings, salary, school fees, travel, or other household costs. This implies either that personal expenses are genuinely zero — implausible — or that personal costs are being absorbed elsewhere, most likely by a partner or from unmodelled savings consumption. If Winchester underperforms, the nursing income becomes the adjustment variable, but the adjustment runs in the wrong direction for clinic recovery. If nursing hours must increase to cover personal shortfalls, this directly reduces available treatment hours at Winchester. The model shows 154 treatment slots monthly based on 7 practitioner hours daily across 22 working days. Every additional nursing shift taken to shore up personal finances removes those hours from Winchester capacity. If conservative occupancy of 30% generates 46 filled slots monthly, and the practitioner adds one 12-hour nursing shift weekly to compensate for household cash needs, this eliminates approximately 12 hours of potential clinic time monthly — a 7.8% capacity reduction that may not matter at 30% occupancy but becomes binding as the clinic attempts to scale toward 70%.

The trigger point is calculable. If Winchester occupancy stalls below 45% (the break-even point of 70 slots generating £16,097 gross revenue), and the conservative scenario monthly loss of £2,993-4,678 begins consuming the £32,500 runway savings, the decision matrix is as follows. At a combined net of £2,722 monthly (month one conservative), monthly savings consumption after covering the loss equals £0 — Bedhampton covers Winchester bleeding with margin to spare. But if Bedhampton revenue drops by even 15% to £8,500 monthly (plausible given that the founder's attention is now split), combined net falls to approximately £1,000 monthly, meaning personal expenses must come from somewhere. If personal expenses run £2,500 monthly (a conservative estimate for any adult in Southern England), the savings runway drops from 99 months to approximately 13 months. If launch costs run to the high estimate of £187,243 rather than the selected £81,786, the savings are already exhausted before month one opens.

The 48-hour decision therefore is not "how do we fix marketing" — it is "do we understand the interdependencies well enough to avoid reflexive cuts that make recovery impossible." Cutting marketing to save £600 extends runway by three weeks while guaranteeing that month-six occupancy cannot reach 70%. Increasing nursing shifts solves personal cash flow while capping Winchester's scalability. Reducing stock quality lowers the average client value below £230, meaning the entire revenue model must be recalculated with lower break-even occupancy becoming impossible rather than easier. The playbook is not a list of actions — it is a sequence that must be executed in order, with marketing investment protected, nursing hours ringfenced for clinic availability, and cost cuts limited to genuine waste rather than investment in future capacity. The founder who panics and cuts everything in month two will save approximately £800 while ensuring that month twelve looks identical to month one.", "4": "The single most likely failure mode for this expansion is not a demand problem, a cost blowout, or a competitive shock. It is the quiet, cumulative erosion of Bedhampton revenue during the Winchester ramp, which the model treats as a stable £7,400 monthly profit throughout all twelve months of every scenario but which in practice will deteriorate the moment the founder's time, attention, and physical presence shifts to the new site. The model assumes Bedhampton continues generating £10,000 gross and £7,400 net without interruption while simultaneously assuming Winchester reaches 70% occupancy requiring 108 treatment slots per month — slots that can only be filled by the same sole practitioner who currently delivers all of Bedhampton's revenue. This is not a conservative assumption being stress-tested; it is a structural impossibility embedded in the base case.

The trigger event occurs before Winchester even opens. During the two months of pre-opening rent (offset by three rent-free months, netting to a £-2,708 pre-opening property cost), the founder will be absorbed in fit-out, regulatory compliance, supplier negotiations, and the 112 launch tasks of which only 2 are currently complete. Bedhampton clients will begin experiencing reduced availability, longer booking lead times, and cancelled or rescheduled appointments. The 60% repeat booking rate that sustains Bedhampton's £10,000 monthly gross depends on consistent access to the practitioner; once that access degrades, clients do not wait — they find alternatives. By November 2026, the month Winchester opens, Bedhampton will likely have already lost 10-15% of its monthly gross, reducing net profit from £7,400 to approximately £6,100-£6,600.

Month 1 of Winchester operations is projected at 32.5% occupancy generating £11,512 gross and a net loss of £2,508. Combined with a now-degraded Bedhampton at £6,300 net, the true combined position is approximately £3,792 — not the £4,892 shown. The founder will interpret this as launch friction, not as the beginning of a structural problem. Month 2 projects Winchester at 40% occupancy and £14,168 gross, with a Winchester loss of £1,055 and combined net of £6,345. But if Bedhampton has now fallen to £5,800 net due to continued neglect — clients booking elsewhere, reduced capacity for new enquiries — the real combined figure is closer to £4,745. The model still shows cumulative P&L of £11,237; reality is trending toward £8,500.

Month 3 is where the trajectory locks in. Winchester is supposed to reach 47.5% occupancy and cross into profitability at £398 net. But the founder is now splitting time between two sites, and Bedhampton — generating zero rent, zero staffing costs, zero insurance in the model because these are treated as non-existent — is actually generating reduced gross revenue as clients attrite. If Bedhampton gross falls to £8,500, variable costs at 26% consume £2,210, leaving net of £6,290 rather than £7,400. Combined net is now £6,688 rather than £7,798. Cumulative P&L is £15,188 rather than £19,035. The gap is £3,847 — not catastrophic, but the slope is wrong.

Month 4 is the last decision point where the trajectory can be changed at acceptable cost. Winchester net is £1,851; Bedhampton, if still declining, might be at £5,600 net. Combined is £7,451 versus the modelled £9,251. The founder has now burned through approximately £12,000-£15,000 of the £32,500 runway savings on launch costs and operating shortfalls. The decision available at this moment is to pause Winchester marketing, halt further fit-out spending, and return full-time to Bedhampton to arrest the client attrition before it becomes permanent. The cost of this decision is approximately £8,805 per month in Winchester fixed costs for a mothballed site — roughly three months of preservation at £26,415 before a restart decision must be made. The founder will not make this decision because Month 4 still shows positive combined cash flow, and positive cash flow feels like progress.

Month 5 and Month 6 continue the ramp. Winchester reaches £22,138 and £24,794 gross respectively. It is a permanent reduction in the value of the enterprise, caused by treating Bedhampton as a fixed income stream rather than as a client base that requires active maintenance, and by modelling a sole practitioner's capacity as infinitely divisible across two geographically separate locations.", "5": "The model allocates £600 per month to marketing as a shared dual-clinic expense, which means Winchester's effective advertising budget is approximately £300 per month assuming equal split. The second vulnerable profile is the multi-service clinic offering aesthetics alongside beauty treatments, nails, or semi-permanent makeup. column4: title
column18: linked_risk_ids

column4: Service charge uncapped and undisclosed
column5: Commercial leases often include variable service charges that can increase significantly year on year. If the charge is uncapped in the lease, Abi has no protection against escalation. The financial model uses a fixed monthly cost assumption that may prove materially wrong.

column4: Rateable value at £31,250 producing rates bill of £1,332/month vs £996 modelled
column5: The confirmed rateable value of 9A Jewry Street is £31,250 — above the small business rates relief threshold. The actual monthly rates bill is approximately £1,332, which is £336 higher than the £996 modelled. This difference flows directly to the bottom line and could push Winchester into loss in early months.

column4: Bedhampton soft patch during Winchester ramp removes financial bridge
column5: The model assumes Bedhampton continues generating ~£8,800/month to bridge Winchester through its ramp-up phase. A seasonal dip, client loss, or practitioner absence at Bedhampton during the critical months Oct–Dec 2026 would erode the bridge and accelerate cash depletion.

column4: ACV of £255 not achieved at Winchester launch
column5: The financial model uses an average client value of £255. Winchester is a new, unproven location with no existing client base. Early clients may book lower-value introductory treatments, suppressing ACV during ramp. If actual ACV comes in at £180–200, break-even recedes by 2–3 months.

column4: Fit-out contractor (Abi's dad) unavailable or delayed
column5: The fit-out plan relies on Abi's father as the primary contractor for labour, saving an estimated £15,000–25,000. If he becomes unavailable due to health, competing commitments, or other reasons, a commercial contractor would need to be engaged at short notice, materially increasing costs and potentially delaying the opening date.

column4: Adverse event at Winchester with no documented SOP
column5: An adverse clinical event (e.g. vascular occlusion, anaphylaxis) at a new clinic with no documented Standard Operating Procedure, no crash kit confirmed on site, and no pre-established escalation protocol would be clinically dangerous and could result in CQC investigation, indemnity claim, or NMC referral.

column4: Abi unable to work — illness, injury, no locum plan
column5: Abi is the sole revenue-generating practitioner across both clinics. Extended absence due to illness, injury, or family emergency with no locum arrangement would immediately halt Winchester revenue and potentially Bedhampton revenue simultaneously. The model carries no locum cost assumption.

column4: School run coverage breakdown on clinic day
column5: The Life Design plan requires school run coverage on Winchester clinic days. If the agreed arrangement fails (e.g. David unavailable, childminder change), Abi may need to cancel clients at short notice. Repeat occurrences damage client retention and reputation in a cold-start location.

column4: Listed building consent required for signage
column5: 9A Jewry Street sits in Winchester City Centre Conservation Area and is adjacent to Grade II listed buildings. Any external signage is likely to require Advertisement Consent. If Listed Building Consent is also needed, the approval timeline extends significantly and the permitted design options are constrained.

column4: Winchester cold start — zero client transfer from Bedhampton
column5: The model assumes some client migration from Bedhampton to Winchester. If no existing clients are willing or able to travel, Winchester opens with zero baseline revenue and must acquire every client from cold. The ramp profile would extend by 2–4 months, significantly increasing cash burn.
column12: Winchester opens with no guaranteed local client base. Every booking in month 1 must be acquired from cold, extending the ramp profile and increasing cash burn if acquisition underperforms. The twelve-week pre-launch marketing campaign is the primary mitigation — it must be planned, funded, and live before the lease is signed, not after. David to build the Winchester launch campaign structure by 1 August 2026 covering META ads, Google Ads, Google Business Profile, email campaign to existing Bedhampton client base, and soft launch event. Set a minimum bookings target for opening week and track weekly against it from November 1. If bookings in weeks 1-2 are tracking below target, increase ad spend immediately from the reserved marketing budget rather than waiting for month-end review.

column4: VAT treatment of aesthetics treatments not confirmed by accountant
column5: The VAT status of aesthetic treatments is complex — some qualify as exempt medical services, others are standard-rated cosmetic treatments. Without a written opinion from a healthcare-specialist accountant, there is a risk of under-collecting VAT or incorrectly exempting supplies. HMRC penalties can be retrospective.
column12: All treatments are treated as standard-rated at 20% VAT with no exemptions claimed. This is consistent with current UK aesthetics legislation where medical exemption arguments are largely unsupported in practice. The financial model already reflects full 20% VAT with no offset — worst case is modelled and accepted. No further action required until legislation changes or HMRC issues updated guidance. Review annually or if regulatory position changes.

column4: Nursing exit delayed beyond October 2026
column5: The plan assumes Abi exits nursing employment by October 2026. Delayed exit extends the dual-income overlap but also delays full clinical availability at Winchester. If notice period is longer than modelled, Winchester opening days per week may be constrained during the most critical ramp months.

column4: CQC registration required sooner than anticipated
column5: If Winchester's treatment scope expands to include services that trigger CQC registration (e.g. regulated medical activities), or if regulatory guidance changes, Abi may need CQC registration before opening. CQC applications typically take 3–6 months and cannot be rushed.

column4: Indemnity insurance not updated to cover Winchester before opening
column5: Abi's current Hamilton Fraser indemnity covers Bedhampton only. If Winchester opens without the policy being updated to include 9A Jewry Street as a second premises, any clinical incident at Winchester will not be covered. This must be actioned on lease completion day.

column4: Prescription record-keeping not configured before first client
column5: Human Medicines Regulations 2012 require all prescriptions to be recorded with specific fields and retained for a minimum of 2 years. If the ANS prescription module or equivalent is not configured and tested before the first client at Winchester, Abi is in technical breach from day one.

column4: JCCP not registered before opening
column5: JCCP registration is a professional credibility requirement and increasingly expected by clients booking injectable treatments. Failure to register before Winchester opens means Abi cannot be listed on the JCCP register, which affects client trust and may affect insurer expectations.

column4: Premium pricing above market median suppresses cold-start conversion
column5: Winchester's target ACV of £255 is above the median for the local aesthetics market. At cold start, with no local reputation or word of mouth, price-sensitive clients may choose established competitors offering similar treatments at lower prices. The ramp profile may be slower than modelled.

column4: Competitor aggressive promotion targeting same demographic at launch
column5: A competitor clinic in Winchester could respond to Abi's launch with aggressive promotional activity — discounting, launch offers, or targeted social media spend — to retain clients who might otherwise transfer. This is particularly likely if a local competitor perceives Winchester as a direct threat.

column4: VAT threshold breach within 1–2 months of opening
column5: The business is already close to the £90k VAT registration threshold. Winchester revenue could push it over within weeks of opening, requiring immediate VAT registration. This would add 20% to all cosmetic treatment prices or absorb cost directly, materially affecting the financial model.
column12: Without proactive VAT planning, Winchester revenue could push the business over the £90k threshold within weeks of opening, adding 20% to all standard-rated treatment prices or absorbing the cost directly into margin. Instruct accountant to confirm VAT liability position before lease signing, establish which treatments are standard-rated vs exempt, register proactively before the threshold is hit, and review Winchester pricing under a VAT-registered position before opening.

column4: No break clause secured in lease
column5: If heads of terms are agreed without a break clause, Abi is locked into a full lease term with no exit option. If Winchester underperforms or personal circumstances change, the only exits are assignment, subletting, or surrender — all complex and costly. A break at year 3 is non-negotiable.
column12: Without a break clause, Winchester underperformance after year 3 locks the business into a full 5-year term with no exit - potential exposure of £64,000+ in rent with no viable way out short of assignment or surrender. Break clause at year 3 on 6 months' written notice has been included as a condition in the offer letter submitted to Thomas Siddall, Goadsby, on 17 May 2026. Solicitor to ensure this is captured explicitly in heads of terms and not conceded during negotiation. This is a non-negotiable condition of proceeding.

column4: Competing offers accepted before heads of terms agreed
column5: 9A Jewry Street is an attractive Winchester High Street unit. Delay in instructing solicitors or stalling during due diligence could allow another party to secure the space. Once another party reaches heads of terms, re-entry to negotiate is very difficult.
column12: Loss of 9a Jewry Street to a competing offer before heads of terms are agreed would require restarting the property search entirely, delaying the November 2026 opening target and extending the period during which Bedhampton must sustain the business alone. Offer letter and covenant pack submitted to Thomas Siddall, Goadsby, on 17 May 2026. David to follow up with Thomas by phone within 48 hours of sending to confirm receipt, gauge landlord response, and signal urgency without appearing desperate. Solicitor already identified and ready to instruct immediately on preferred bidder status being confirmed.

column4: October 2026 cash low point — fit-out costs peak with zero Winchester revenue
column5: October 2026 is the projected cash low point: fit-out spend is at its peak while Winchester has not yet opened and generates no revenue. Bedhampton revenue alone must cover all costs. Any cost overrun or Bedhampton shortfall in this window could exhaust the runway buffer.
column12: October 2026 is the highest cash exposure month. Fit-out spend is at its peak, Winchester has not yet opened, and Bedhampton net income alone must cover all business costs including the first month of Winchester rent. A cost overrun, delayed fit-out, or Bedhampton soft month in this window could materially compress the cash buffer. Build a detailed month-by-month cash flow for August-November 2026 tracking all known outflows against confirmed Bedhampton income. Identify the minimum cash reserve threshold that must be maintained entering October. Ensure fit-out materials are ordered and staged to avoid lumpy October spend where possible. David to monitor Bedhampton bookings weekly from August and flag immediately if revenue tracking below £8,500/month.

column4: Steve — Parkers Design (Southern) Ltd — £2,419 + VAT (£2,902.80 inc VAT) discounted upfront / £3,024 + VAT (£3,628.80 inc VAT) standard terms — 9A Jewry Street SO23 8RZ Architectural & Planning Package (VAT standard rate)
column9:
Comprehensive architectural technician package for proposed medical aesthetics clinic fit-out at 9A Jewry Street, Winchester SO23 8RZ. Scope includes measured 3D lidar survey, existing and proposed layouts, planning application preparation and management, signage/frontage support, heritage/conservation support if required, Building Regulations package, 3D visuals/renders and unlimited reasonable amendments prior to planning decision. Internal remodelling includes 3 treatment rooms, reception and convenience area. Conservation area status acknowledged explicitly within quote.

Commercially this is currently the strongest value-aligned proposal reviewed. Scope is materially more operationally focused than KPD and notably more complete than several cheaper survey-only providers. Parkers clearly understand the distinction between planning approval and Building Regulations delivery and explicitly include Building Regulations documentation within pricing, which many competitors exclude. Their wording and example packs suggest a practical contractor-oriented workflow rather than purely planning-stage architecture.

Strong positives:
• Fixed pricing structure with unlimited reasonable amendments before decision.
• Includes Building Regulations package.
• Explicit conservation/planning awareness.
• Price-match willingness on like-for-like written quotes.
• Evening/weekend availability noted in T&Cs.
• Strong example drawing pack quality with detailed layouts, elevations, sections and 3D visuals shown in supplied examples.
• Practical/referral-oriented approach to builders and contractors rather than “architecture prestige” positioning.
• Military discount already applied within pricing structure.

Commercial assessment:
Parkers appear to sit in the sweet spot between:

practical architectural technicians
planning support
contractor usability
commercial cost efficiency

rather than over-indexing toward architectural branding/prestige. For a relatively straightforward internal commercial clinic fit-out within an existing retail shell, this is likely commercially rational.

Key flags:
(1) They are architectural technicians rather than RIBA architects. This is unlikely to matter operationally unless planning/conservation becomes unusually contentious.
(2) DWG/CAD files are not included and cost £500 extra if later required.
(3) Building Regulations submission itself is not handled by Parkers — client must appoint Building Control separately.
(4) Structural calculations excluded if structural works emerge.
(5) Their T&Cs are heavily weighted commercially in their favour after survey/drawing commencement. 50% payable after survey stage even if project withdrawn; 100% payable after first draft issue.

Strategic observation:
Unlike KPD, Parkers’ material feels less polished from a luxury branding perspective but substantially more grounded in actual build/project execution. The supplied examples show genuinely detailed and technically usable drawing packs rather than purely marketing visuals.

Current positioning versus competitors:
• Better value and more operationally aligned than KPD.
• More complete and commercially reassuring than basic survey-only firms.
• Potentially the best balance currently reviewed between cost, practical delivery and planning/build compliance.

Status: Current leading contender pending:
• clarification of realistic delivery timeline
• confirmation of commercial/clinic fit-out experience
• confirmation measured layouts will include accurate contractor-grade dimensions
• review of whether DWG release can be negotiated into package.

Title: Gerrard Conway — Keenan Project Designs Ltd (RIBA & ARB) — £5,000 + VAT (£6,000 inc VAT) — Medical Aesthetics Clinic Planning Ref 511 (VAT standard rate)

Notes: Architectural planning and design package for proposed medical aesthetics clinic at 9A Jewry Street, Winchester. Scope includes measured site survey, consultation call with architect, existing and proposed floor plans, proposed elevations, site maps, planning submission drawings, free external 3D visuals, free material list estimate, planning submission handling and council liaison. Includes free drawing alterations for first three months after first draft. Building Regulations drawings explicitly excluded. Structural calculations excluded. CAD/DWG editable files explicitly excluded — PDF issue only.

Fee structure fixed at £5,000 ex VAT / £6,000 inc VAT. Payment structure: 30% deposit upfront, 70% due on first drawing issue. Drawings released with watermark until full balance paid. Deposit becomes non-refundable once outline work commences and project cannot be cancelled after site visit. All invoices payable within 7 days with “no exclusions” to payment terms.

KPD position themselves as a nationwide award-winning RIBA and ARB architectural practice with experience across residential and commercial projects. Marketing heavily emphasises awards, planning success rate, volume of projects and Google reviews. Offices listed in Royal Leamington Spa and Marble Arch London.

Commercial assessment:
Fee level is broadly reasonable for measured survey + planning-stage architectural package. However, this is not a full technical delivery or fit-out management package. Their scope appears heavily weighted toward planning drawings and submission rather than contractor coordination, clinical fit-out detailing, M&E integration, or construction-stage support.

Five flags:
(1) Building Regulations package excluded. This is a major downstream cost/risk area for a clinic fit-out and likely requires additional consultant fees later.
(2) CAD/DWG files not released. This creates dependency on KPD for future revisions and may complicate contractor, engineer or fit-out coordination later.
(3) Structural calculations excluded and separately chargeable if required.
(4) Payment structure strongly protects KPD commercially — especially non-refundable deposit and watermark withholding until final payment.
(5) Scope does not clearly include construction-stage technical detailing, contractor tender coordination, M&E drawings, or commercial healthcare compliance support.

Strategic observation:
This appears to be a competent planning-stage architecture provider rather than a specialist commercial clinic fit-out architect. Likely suitable for landlord/planning/conservation approval package if managed correctly, but potentially insufficient alone for full commercial delivery without additional technical consultants or later-stage drawing packages.

Status: Quote received. Do not appoint until confirming:
• who will actually lead the project day-to-day
• whether they can provide Building Regulations and technical fit-out packages later
• whether CAD files can be licensed/released
• recent comparable conservation-area commercial projects
• realistic delivery timelines and revision process.

column4: FMB Contractor Enquiry Response — Indicative Fit-Out Cost Feedback for 9A Jewry Street Medical Aesthetics Clinic
column9:
Early-stage contractor feedback received via FMB enquiry regarding proposed 614 sq ft (57m²) CQC aesthetics clinic fit-out at 9A Jewry Street, Winchester SO23 8RZ. Contractor advised that based on current market conditions and previous comparable healthcare/commercial fit-out projects, realistic fit-out costs would likely fall in the region of:
• ~£1,200/m² ex VAT for a basic conversion/fit-out
• ~£1,500–£1,700/m² ex VAT on similar completed projects

This implies indicative total project costs of approximately:
• ~£68k + VAT at £1,200/m²
• ~£85k–£97k + VAT at £1,500–£1,700/m²

even allowing for family-provided joinery input.

Contractor noted that formal estimating at feasibility stage would normally require paid quantity surveying input and more detailed designed information/specification before accurate pricing could be produced. They indicated willingness to review the project later as a contractor proposal/value-engineering exercise once drawings and quantities exist.

Commercial assessment:
This is one of the more commercially realistic and professionally cautious responses received so far. Importantly, they did not:

lowball pricing to secure engagement
overpromise on budget
pretend feasibility pricing could be accurately produced without drawings/specification

Their response aligns broadly with genuine commercial fit-out market rates rather than optimistic SME pricing assumptions.

However, there is an important contextual nuance:
Their assumed £1,200–£1,700/m² rates are likely based on:

fully contractor-managed delivery
commercial overhead structures
more turnkey/comprehensive fit-outs
less owner-managed procurement
less family/self-delivered labour contribution

That distinction matters significantly.

Strategic observation:
This response does NOT necessarily mean your project is unviable.

It DOES mean:
your current £20k–£25k target is almost certainly too low for a professionally delivered turnkey clinic fit-out if using conventional contractor delivery structures.

However, your actual delivery model is atypical because:
• family joinery/carpentry input exists
• client PM capability exists
• substantial existing M&E already retained
• shell quality already good
• self-managed specialist trades likely
• scope intentionally restrained

Those factors materially reduce likely delivered cost versus benchmark contractor-led commercial fit-outs.

Most important insight:
This response indirectly validates your emerging strategy of:

lean technical design team
self-managed PM structure
selective contractor engagement
retaining existing infrastructure
controlling specification creep

because traditional turnkey delivery routes likely WOULD push the project toward £70k–£100k territory relatively quickly.

Key flags:
(1) Your £20k–£25k working budget remains unrealistic unless scope is tightly controlled and self-managed.
(2) HVAC/fire/acoustic/ceiling compliance requirements remain major unknown cost-risk items.
(3) Full-height partition/fire stopping requirements could materially increase labour and coordination costs.
(4) Contractor-led “commercial rates” escalate rapidly once full coordination and compliance responsibility transfer to builder.

Strategic conclusion:
This response strengthens — rather than weakens — the case for your currently emerging delivery structure:
• self-managed PM
• selective trade procurement
• limited principal contractor role
• retaining existing infrastructure
• avoiding over-specification
• tight scope discipline

Status: Informal feasibility-stage contractor feedback only. No formal quote or engagement proposed at this stage. Valuable primarily as external market-rate benchmarking and budget stress-test input.
